ShortDescription.Value: Real-world car colors in traffic, with sliders to tune the mix and slots for your own custom colors.

Publish.LongDescription:
# What it does
Vanilla traffic in Cities: Skylines II spawns cars from a roughly uniform color palette, so streets end up looking far more colorful than the real world. **Realistic Vehicle Colors** rebalances the spawn weights to match real-world car-color statistics — mostly white, black, grey and silver, with the rest spread across reds, blues, greens and the occasional bright color.
# Features
- **Real-world defaults** for ten color buckets, on by default. - **Per-color sliders** to tune the mix yourself: White, Black, Grey, Silver, Red, Blue, Brown / Beige, Green, Yellow and Other. - **Three custom color slots** — pick any hex code and a probability. Great for adding rare colors (orange, teal, magenta) or making a fleet your own. - **Apply settings live** without restarting the game. Newly spawned vehicles use the new mix immediately. - **Reset color sliders** button restores the recommended real-world mix. - **Camper trailers** are restricted to white and brown to match how they actually look on the road.
# Scope
Affects civilian road vehicles: personal cars, delivery vans, cargo trucks, food-delivery scooters, campers and bicycles.
Service vehicles (police, fire, ambulance, garbage, post, taxi, transit), farm equipment, semi-trailers, trains, ships and airplanes are left at their stock paintwork.
# Notes
- Cars already on the map keep their old color until they despawn — only newly spawned vehicles use the updated mix. Most fleets rotate within a few minutes of in-game time at 3× speed. - Settings persist between sessions in `ModsSettings/RealisticVehicleColors/`.
Publish.ChangeLog:
v1.0 — initial release.
- Real-world color rebalance for civilian vehicles. - Ten per-color sliders + three custom color slots. - Apply settings live without restarting the game. - Reset color sliders to recommended real-world mix. - Camper trailer restriction (white / brown only).
